item.title: $840,700 CWM Hospital upgrade delivers new operating theatre and Fiji's first adolescent ward
item.description: Patients at the Colonial War Memorial Hospital will benefit from improved surgical and paediatric services after the Ministry of Health officially received an $840,700 modernisation project funded by The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints , including a new minor operating theatre and Fiji's first dedicated adolescent ward. Speaking during the official handover, Assistant Minister for Heal
item.content:encoded:
Patients at the Colonial War Memorial Hospital will benefit from improved surgical and paediatric services after the Ministry of Health officially received an $840,700 modernisation project funded by The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints , including a new minor operating theatre and Fiji's first dedicated adolescent ward.

Speaking during the official handover, Assistant Minister for Health Penioni Ravunawa says the project is more than an investment in buildings , describing it as an investment in people, partnerships and the future of healthcare in Fiji.

Ravunawa says Government cannot strengthen the country's health system alone, stressing that partnerships with development partners, faith-based organisations, civil society and the private sector are essential to delivering quality healthcare.

One of the major upgrades is the conversion of a procedure room in the New Surgical Ward into a dedicated Minor Operating Theatre.

He says the 49-bed ward cares for patients with severe diabetic foot sepsis, with diabetic foot surgery accounting for about 70 percent of CWM Hospital's overall surgical workload.

Ravunawa says during the post-COVID recovery period, only four of the hospital's eight operating theatres were operational while the others underwent refurbishment.

He says this meant vulnerable patients often had to be transported across the hospital for surgery, while staff also faced challenges with ageing infrastructure, unreliable lifts, shortages of patient trolleys, linen constraints and intermittent water supply.

Ravunawa says the new operating theatre will allow procedures such as wound debridement, biopsies and central venous line insertions to be carried out within the ward under local anaesthesia and conscious sedation, reducing delays, easing surgical backlogs, improving patient safety and delivering better clinical outcomes.

He also acknowledged Medical Superintendent Dr Luke Nasedra for his leadership in planning and implementing the project.

The Children's Hospital has also undergone significant improvements, including the repair and strengthening of its glass rooftop, which had become a safety concern since 2023.

The upgraded facility now includes a restored Cardiac Echo Scanning Room used to diagnose Rheumatic Heart Disease, additional biomedical rooms and extra storage space.

For the first time, Fiji also has a dedicated adolescent ward with 20 to 24 beds for young people aged between 16 and 19 years.

Ravunawa says adolescents receiving treatment for non-communicable diseases, burns, traumatic injuries and gender-based violence will now be cared for in an environment designed specifically for their physical, emotional and developmental needs instead of being admitted to adult wards.

The project also includes a Child Protection Room for abuse cases, an Infection Prevention and Control Room, a dedicated Burns Unit with a therapeutic bathtub to reduce hospital-acquired infections, and improved accommodation and rest areas for mothers and caregivers.

Meanwhile, Stake President of the Fiji Suva Stake of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints , Panapasa Ratuvukivuki , says the project is about more than renovated buildings and improved facilities, describing it as a celebration of hope, healing and compassionate service.

Ratuvukivuki says the Church is grateful to partner with the Ministry of Health and CWM Hospital to improve healthcare services for the people of Fiji.

He says the investment has enabled various departments within the hospital to be repaired, refreshed and upgraded, creating a safer, more welcoming and dignified environment for patients, their families and healthcare professionals.

Ratuvukivuki says the Church's humanitarian service is deeply rooted in the teachings of Jesus Christ, with caring for those in need viewed as a sacred responsibility.

He says hospitals are places where people face some of life's greatest challenges, and every patient deserves not only competent medical care but also dignity, compassion and respect.

Ratuvukivuki acknowledged the dedication of doctors, nurses, specialists, support staff and administrators, thanking them for their commitment to improving the lives of countless people across Fiji.

He says the Church's humanitarian work extends around the world through support for hospitals and medical facilities, maternal and newborn health programmes, clean water projects, wheelchair distributions, educational opportunities, refugee assistance, food security programmes, disaster relief and community development initiatives.

Ratuvukivuki says the CWM Hospital project is an investment not only in infrastructure but also in human dignity and community wellbeing.

He thanked the Ministry of Health, CWM Hospital management, project teams, contractors and everyone involved in delivering the upgrades.

Ratuvukivuki says the improved facilities will help deliver better healthcare outcomes, enhance the experience of patients and families, and provide healthcare workers with an environment that reflects the value of their service.

Ravunawa thanked The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints for its generosity, saying the investment has permanently improved the standard of care at Fiji's national referral hospital and will benefit patients for many years to come.

He also called on healthcare workers to care for and maintain the new facilities so they continue serving future generations of Fijians, while Ratuvukivuki reaffirmed the Church's commitment to continue partnering with Fiji in projects that alleviate suffering, strengthen communities and bring hope to people across the country.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Defence says FICAC failed to prove perjury charges against Kamikamica, decision on August 3rd
item.description: Former Deputy Prime Minister, Manoa Kamikamica's defence lawyer, Wylie Clarke says the two counts against his client fail now even though FICAC might have proven a Commission process, but it does not prove the elements of either offence. While making his submission on the no case to answer application, Clarke said FICAC's case had already collapsed on Tuesday, as a result of the evidence given by
item.content:encoded:
Former Deputy Prime Minister, Manoa Kamikamica's defence lawyer, Wylie Clarke says the two counts against his client fail now even though FICAC might have proven a Commission process, but it does not prove the elements of either offence.

While making his submission on the no case to answer application, Clarke said FICAC's case had already collapsed on Tuesday, as a result of the evidence given by Janet Mason, the counsel assisting with the COI, and also the evidence that she did not give.

He says no evidence or falsity for the expressly pleaded statement has been shown as the amended charge itself alleges that Kamikamica made a statement which he knew to be false or did not believe to be true.

The defence lawyer says FICAC must prove that allegation, and it did not, and while Mason could prove the commission process, she had no direct knowledge of what Kamikamica knew, believed, or intended.

He says Kalaveti Ravu's diary was admitted only as proof that entries were made, not for the truth of their context, while Ravu gave no other relevant evidence in his proceedings.

Clarke says the 3rd December 2024 letter to Kamikamica did not identify prior knowledge of Barbara Malimali's appointment as an issue.

He says Mason did not put that question directly to Kamikamica and accepted that the Commission did not pursue the issue because it was outside the terms of reference.

In response, FICAC counsel Josann Pene stated that Section 176.1 of the Crimes Act stipulates that any person who willfully makes a statement material in that proceeding, which he or she knows to be false, or does not believe to be true, commits an indictable offence.

Pene says the elements of the offence are the identity of Manoa Kamikamica, the person is often sworn as a witness in a judicial proceeding pertaining to an inquiry into the appointment of the FICAC Commissioner, and that he or she made a material statement claiming that he had no knowledge of the appointment of the FICAC Commissioner prior to the case of an offence pertaining to a criminal case, a nd that he knew the cause but does not believe it to be true.

Magistrate Charles Ratakele will deliver his ruling on 3rd August.

Kamikamica is charged with perjury and giving a false statement to a public servant.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Those concerned over school preaching video should report it to authorities - Rabuka
item.description: People concerned about the viral video showing a preacher addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka should report the matter to the appropriate authorities rather than the Prime Minister. While responding to questions about the incident, Rabuka says those who believe there was any wrongdoing should take their concerns to a gencies such as the Police, the Online Safety Commissi
item.content:encoded:
People concerned about the viral video showing a preacher addressing students at a prominent secondary school in Lautoka should report the matter to the appropriate authorities rather than the Prime Minister.

While responding to questions about the incident, Rabuka says those who believe there was any wrongdoing should take their concerns to a gencies such as the Police, the Online Safety Commission and other relevant authorities, which would then deal with the matter through the proper government processes.

Insert: PM - Take it to the authorities July 23

Rabuka says while the Government establishes laws and policies through Parliament, it is the responsibility of relevant departments and agencies to enforce them.

When asked whether the incident raised constitutional issues, Rabuka questioned the link, saying the focus should remain on whether any laws had been broken.

Meanwhile, a video circulating on social media that appears to show Christian preacher and content creator Osaslugo addressing students during a school session.

In the footage, Osaslugo appears to single out a student identified as Muslim before telling him that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

The video has attracted widespread criticism online, with many expressing concern that the child was publicly singled out because of his religion and subjected to comments that could be viewed as discriminatory and inappropriate in a school setting.

Questions have also been raised about how the preacher was allowed to address students during school hours and whether the school had approved the session.

The Fiji Human Rights and Anti-Discrimination Commission has since called on the Ministry of Education to investigate the incident promptly, saying any situation where a child feels targeted, pressured, humiliated or excluded because of their religion raises significant human rights and child protection concerns.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Only COI expenditure to be tabled in Parliament, not report - Rabuka
item.description: There is no need for the Commission of Inquiry (COI) report to be tabled in Parliament because it was commissioned by the President and is to be submitted to the Head of State. Responding to questions from the media about the release of the report, Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says only the expenditure relating to the Commission of Inquiry will need to be presented to Parliament as part of the G
item.content:encoded:
There is no need for the Commission of Inquiry (COI) report to be tabled in Parliament because it was commissioned by the President and is to be submitted to the Head of State.

Responding to questions from the media about the release of the report, Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says only the expenditure relating to the Commission of Inquiry will need to be presented to Parliament as part of the Government's financial reporting.

Rabuka says any action arising from the report will be carried out by the Office of the Prime Minister, the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (DPP), the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (FICAC) and the Fiji Police Force.

Rabuka says the public has a right to know how much public money was spent on the inquiry and to be informed of its findings and recommendations.

He says the report's recommendations will be referred to the relevant authorities, with the Police forwarding matters to the DPP where necessary, while FICAC would deal with cases that fall within its jurisdiction.

Rabuka also said the inquiry had provided clarity for those who had come under suspicion.

He says at least those people who have been suspected of doing wrong now know that they didn't do anything wrong.

He acknowledged that significant public funds had been spent on the inquiry but says such expenditure was necessary to ensure accountability and uphold the rule of law.

Click here for more stories on the Barbara Malimali suspension and dismissal of AG Graham Leung

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: FCCC warns businesses against limiting sugar purchases
item.description: Businesses are being warned they could face enforcement action if they restrict consumers from buying the quantity of sugar they need. This was highlighted by the FCCC, saying retailers and traders must not impose purchase limits or ration the amount of sugar a customer can buy , as doing so may breach Section 87(I) of the FCCC Act 2010.
item.content:encoded:
Businesses are being warned they could face enforcement action if they restrict consumers from buying the quantity of sugar they need.

This was highlighted by the FCCC, saying retailers and traders must not impose purchase limits or ration the amount of sugar a customer can buy , as doing so may breach Section 87(I) of the FCCC Act 2010.

The warning comes as the FCCC's enforcement officers continue inspections across the country to assess market conditions relating to the rationing and conditional selling of sugar at the retail level.

The FCCC says its teams are checking bulk storage facilities, verifying purchase orders, reviewing distribution patterns and monitoring supply chain movements, including the frequency of sugar deliveries and replenishment schedules.

The teams are also engaging with retailers, traders and businesses to establish expected stock availability and how long current and anticipated sugar supplies are expected to remain on shelves.

The Commission says while genuine supply disruptions can happen, businesses cannot introduce their own rationing measures with retailers are not permitted to advertise or sell sugar with conditions that limit the quantity a customer can purchase.

Where stock is available and a consumer requests a quantity of sugar while offering the correct payment, the trader must not refuse or fail to supply the requested amount.

Businesses are also reminded that conditional selling is prohibited.

This includes situations where a product is only supplied if a customer buys another product or spends a minimum amount.

The FCCC says compulsory tie-in sales or bundling arrangements that force consumers to purchase additional goods may also breach Section 87(E) of the FCCC Act 2010.

The Commission says sugar remains a price-controlled item, and retail prices must be assessed, reviewed and approved by the FCCC under the Fijian Competition and Consumer Commission (Price Control) (Food Item Prices) Order 2024.

It says any trader importing sugar must submit an application to the FCCC before selling the product to consumers, in accordance with Section 44 of the FCCC Act 2010.

The FCCC says any breaches of the Act may be treated as a statutory offence and could result in formal investigations and enforcement action.

Businesses have been advised to review their current sales practices, including policies on bulk purchases and promotional bundles, and ensure frontline staff are properly trained to avoid non-compliance.

Consumers who experience overcharging or unfair trading practices can report these matters to the FCCC by calling 8921991 or emailing helpdesk@fccc.gov.fj .

Consumers can also contact the FCCC's Western Division office on 8911623, Sigatoka residents on 9982437, or the Northern Division office on 8911624.
